title: L05.1.modules intro
body:
Toolkit for Finance and Financial Decision Making
Lecture 5.1: Modules and packages

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin
UNSW




                                                    1
 Overview




    Modules and Packages
       Creating a Module
       Accessing functionality from other modules
       Including test cases and examples using __name__
    Docstrings and Comments
    IO and Filepaths
       Filepaths
       IO and Filepaths
       Best Practice




                                                           2
 Modules and packages
 Modules and packages




 Modules are an Python object that serves as an organisation unit:

      A module is any file of Python code.
          For example, PyCharm’s default Python project includes a file called main.py. Thus, the code in
           this file resides in a module called main.
          If you were working on a Finance project, you might group code related to stock tickers in a file
           called ticker.py.

      Code in each module is distinct.
          Modules have a namespace containing all objects defined in the module
          Code in one module is not automatically accessible in another – an import statement is required.
          Importing the module provides access to the defined objects.




                                                                                                               3
 Modules and packages




 Packages are collections of interrelated modules grouped under a shared package namespace.

      Modules defined inside a package have their own namespace, which is nested within the
      package namespace.
      The presence of a special __init__.py file (an __init__ module) informs Python that the
      directory is a package.
      Importing the packages allows us to access the contained modules

              While we will use packages in the class, we will not discuss creating a package.




                                                                                                 4
 Modules: Creation
 Modules: Creation


     Recall our bank withdrawal code:
1        def withdrawal(account_balance, withdrawal_amount, cash_in_wallet):
2
3            if withdrawal_amount <= account_balance:
4                account_balance = account_balance - withdrawal_amount
5                cash_in_wallet = cash_in_wallet + withdrawal_amount
6                print("Withdrawal successful.")
7            else:
8                print("Insufficient funds. Withdrawal denied.")
9
10           return account_balance, cash_in_wallet
11
12       account_balance = 500.00         # Current balance in dollars
13       cash_in_wallet = 100.00          # Current cash on hand
14
15       account_balance, cash_in_wallet = withdrawal(account_balance, 50, cash_in_wallet)
16       account_balance, cash_in_wallet = withdrawal(account_balance, 75, cash_in_wallet)
17       account_balance, cash_in_wallet = withdrawal(account_balance, 125, cash_in_wallet)
18
19       print(f"Final account balance: ${account_balance:.2f}")
20       print(f"Final cash in wallet: ${cash_in_wallet:.2f}")


                 A module is the best way to make sure we can use this function in different files.



                                                                                                      5
 Modules: Creation




     Save the function as automated_teller_machine.py

      1. Open PyCharm and right-click on the toolkit project folder.
      2. Choose New » Python File
      3. Name this file automated_teller_machine.py
      4. Open this module in PyCharm and copy the function below:
1       def withdrawal(account_balance, withdrawal_amount, cash_in_wallet):
2
3            if withdrawal_amount <= account_balance:
4                account_balance = account_balance - withdrawal_amount
5                cash_in_wallet = cash_in_wallet + withdrawal_amount
6                print("Withdrawal successful.")
7            else:
8                print("Insufficient funds. Withdrawal denied.")
9
10           return account_balance, cash_in_wallet




                                                                              6
 Modules: Creation




 Execute automated_teller_machine.py and nothing will happen:

     PyCharm will tell you that the code finished with exit code 0 (meaning, no errors).
     The module only defines the function.
     It does not do anything with the function.




                                                                                           7
 Modules: Accessing functionality from other modules
 Modules: Accessing functionality from other modules




    We need to import our module before using it:

    Start a new interactive Python session by opening the PyCharm console and then type:
1       >>> withdrawal(500, 50, 100)
2       NameError: name 'withdrawal' is not defined

    Python raises an exception:
         The name withdrawal is not available in either the global or built-in namespaces.
         We need to tell Python to access withdrawal from a module




                                                                                             8
 Modules: Accessing functionality from other modules




 We access other modules through import statements.

 To import an entire module:
      import <module_name>
         Brings the module namespace into scope as <module_name>
         Access functions and classes using dot notation: <module_name>.function()
      import <module_name> as <alias>
         Brings the entire module namespace into scope as the <alias>
         Access functions and classes using using dot notation under the alias: <alias>.function()

 To import specific functions or classes:
      from <module_name> import item1[, item2]
         Brings the item(s) referenced into scope in the current namespace
         Access items directly as item1. Dot notation is not required.
         Multiple items may be specified with a comma-separated list of names
         Using a wildcard * to bring in all items in the module is not recommended. This can lead to
           name conflicts and unclear code.




                                                                                                        9
 Modules: Accessing functionality from other modules




    We can use our withdrawal function by:

    1. Importing the module and using a qualified name:

1       >>> import automated_teller_machine
2       automated_teller_machine.withdrawal(500, 50, 100)


    2. Importing the module with an alias and using a qualified name:
1       >>> import automated_teller_machine as atm
2       atm.withdrawal(500, 50, 100)


    3. Importing the name from module and using an unqualified name:
1       >>> from automated_teller_machine import withdrawal
2       withdrawal(500, 50, 100)




                                                                        10
 Aside: Tips on the Python console




 The Python console is useful for testing code. Occasionally, you will need to reset it:

      Press the little curved arrow (Rerun) on the left of the console tab.
      Resetting a session reset the global namespace; all names defined in the previous session
      (including imported modules) will not be available unless they are re-imported.




                                                                                                  11
 Modules: Testing and __name__
 Modules: Testing and __name__




 We should test every single function or module we define.

      There are multiple approaches for testing user-defined functions.
      The easiest option is to include a small example inside the relevant modules.
      In a large project, using a testing framework such as PyTest is recommended.


              This courses focuses on the simplest solution, placing test code inside modules.




                                                                                                 12
 Modules: Testing and __name__




     Any test code added directly to a module will be executed whenever the module is imported:

     Add the following test code to the end of the automated_teller_machine module:
 1       balance = 500.00     # Initial balance in dollars
 2       cash = 100.00      # Initial cash on hand
 3
 4       balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 50, cash)
 5       balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 75, cash)
 6       balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 125, cash)
 7       balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 1000, cash)
 8
 9       print(f"Final account balance: ${balance}. Correct value is 250.")
10       print(f"Final cash:            ${cash}.    Correct value is 350.")



      Whenever the module is imported, Python will print the test cash final account balance and cash values.




                                                                                                                13
 Modules: Testing and __name__




 Python has a __name__ variable that indicates whether a module was executed or imported.

      Executed:
         When run directly, __name__ = "__main__" inside the module.
         e.g., when running automated_teller_machine.py, the value of __name__ inside the module is
           the string "__main__".

      Imported:
         When imported: __name__ is set to the module’s name without the extension inside the module
         e.g., when importing automated_teller_machine.py, the value of __name__ inside the module
           is the string "automated_teller_machine".




                                                                                                        14
 Modules: Testing and __name__




     We can check if __name__ == "__main__" and ensure that the tests are performed only when we execute
     the module directly.

     Change the end of automated_teller_machine.py to:
 1       # Example
 2       if __name__ == "__main__":
 3           balance = 500.00     # Initial balance in dollars
 4           cash = 100.00      # Initial cash on hand
 5
 6           balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 50, cash)
 7           balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 75, cash)
 8           balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 125, cash)
 9           balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 1000, cash)
10
11           print(f"Final account balance: ${balance}. Correct value is 250.")
12           print(f"Final cash:            ${cash}.    Correct value is 350.")




                                                                                                           15
